---
#title: "Drone - Willow - Bumblebee"
lightbox: false
---

::: {.column-page}

::: {.grid}

::: {.g-col-6}
## The Drone-Willow-Bumblebee Project
In the rugged mountains of Western Norway, two species of willow (*Salix lanata* and *Salix lapponum*) play a quiet but vital role the life of bumblebees. As climate change reshapes mountain landscapes, understanding how these willow shrubs are spreading or retreating is becoming increasingly important. This project combines drone photography, field surveys, and artificial intelligence in a pilot study to attempt to automatically detect and map willow bushes across the Finse area. 

By training a deep learning model to recognize willows from high-resolution aerial images, we should be able to survey vast and remote areas far faster than would ever be possible on foot. Although, gathering training data still involves a lot of [manual identification](willow-identification.qmd) and hiking in the gorgeous Finse mountain region. This field work and research can open the door to large-scale monitoring of these ecologically important shrubs for years to come.
The project is a cross disciplinary pilot study, in collaboration with the [PPF-Alpine](https://mountainsinmotion.w.uib.no/) group and XXXX. Last autumn, two field campaigns were successfully executed and. 

Have a look at the [various topics](themes.qmd) we present here. See a map of the field work area of Finse below!
:::

::: {.g-col-6}
![Field work group, July(?) 2025](media/team_drone.JPG)

![Finse research station 'live' [webcam](https://www.finse.uio.no/news/webcam/), updated near-daily some time between 6:00 and 18:00. Can you spot any willow bushes?](https://www.finse.uio.no/news/webcam/dagens.jpg)
:::

:::

:::

::: {.my-5}
:::

```{r}
#| echo: false
#| column: screen-inset-shaded
#| message: false

library(leaflet)
library(sf)

# Load the shapefiles
zones <- st_read("media/field_work_shapefiles/squares.shp", quiet = TRUE) %>% 
  st_transform(4326) %>%
  st_zm()
wires <- st_read("media/field_work_shapefiles/wire.shp", quiet = TRUE) %>%
  st_transform(4326) %>%
  st_zm()
fishnet <- st_read("media/field_work_shapefiles/fishnet_100m.shp", quiet = TRUE) %>%
  st_transform(4326) %>%
  st_zm()



leaflet() %>%
  addTiles() %>%  # Add default OpenStreetMap map tiles 
  addMarkers(lng=7.5244779006725375, lat=60.59364220037374, popup="Finse Alpine Resarch Station") %>%
addPolygons(
            data = zones,
            fillColor = "pink",
            fillOpacity = 0.4,
            color = "blue",
            weight = 2,
            popup = ~paste("Field Zone:", Name, "<br>Area:", round(Shape_Area, 0), "m²")
) %>%
addPolylines(
            data = wires,
            color = "red",
            weight = 2
) %>%
addPolylines(
             data = fishnet,
             color = "gray",
             weight = 1
)
```
